query: What is the Buddhist concept of nirvana?
response: Nirvana in Buddhism is often described as the ["quiet place"](https://oceanlibrary.com/dhammapada_buddha?paraId=para_423) where one experiences the cessation of natural desires and finds true happiness, *The Dhammapada*. It is the ultimate goal of the Buddhist path, representing the extinction of self and the end of suffering. The Buddha taught that Nirvana is reached through understanding the Four Noble Truths, which include the existence and cessation of sorrow, and following the Eightfold Path that leads to this cessation, *Sutra Collection (M, Part-2)*. This state is beyond ordinary perception and is not simply annihilation, but rather a profound liberation from the cycle of birth and rebirth, *Sutra Collection (L)*.

query: How do different religions view the afterlife?
response:
Different religions offer diverse perspectives on the afterlife. In Islam, the Qur'án emphasizes that ["every soul shall taste of death"](https://oceanlibrary.com/quran_rodwell-tr?paraId=para_292) and will be judged, with the righteous entering ["gardens with palaces, beneath which the rivers flow"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sura-xxix-the-spider_muhammad?paraId=para_27). Christianity similarly speaks of resurrection, where Jesus promises that ["he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-john?paraId=para_425), highlighting eternal life through faith.

In Sikhism, the Guru Granth Sahib describes the soul's journey through ["countless incarnations in doubt"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20186), emphasizing the importance of spiritual practice in this life. Buddhism, on the other hand, focuses on Nirvāṇa as ["the highest happiness"](https://oceanlibrary.com/dhammapada_buddha?paraId=para_230), a state beyond the cycle of birth and death.

Hinduism, through the Bhagavad Gita, teaches that the soul is ["indestructible"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bhagavad-gita?paraId=para_30) and eternal, transcending the physical body. The Bahá'í Faith views the afterlife as a continuation of the soul's journey, where it progresses in the ["world of Eternal Existence"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16714), free from the limitations of the physical world.

Judaism holds that the spirit returns to God, as expressed in the phrase ["the dust returns to the earth as it was, and the spirit returns to God who gave it"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18208). Taoism sees life and death as part of a continuous cycle, where wisdom lies in recognizing the "immutable continuity" of existence. Each tradition offers a unique lens through which to view the mystery of life beyond death.

response:

Judaism echoes this sentiment with the commandment to ["love your neighbor as yourself"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18347), *Jerusalem Talmud Nedarim*, which is considered a great principle in the Torah.

In Islam, the Qur'án advises believers to respond to greetings with a better or at least equal greeting, illustrating the principle of reciprocity: ["If ye are greeted with a greeting, then greet ye with a better greeting, or at least return it"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sura-iv-women_muhammad/?paraId=para_50), *Sura IV. The Bahá'í Faith expands on this by urging individuals to be ["a cause of healing for every sick one; a comforter for every sorrowful one"](https://siftersearch.com/document/11408), *Dwight Barstow Collection*, emphasizing unity and service. Buddhism, while not explicitly stating the Golden Rule, embodies it through teachings on compassion and mindfulness, encouraging actions that reflect kindness and consideration for all beings. Each tradition, in its own way, upholds the value of empathy and mutual respect as foundational to spiritual life.

query: How do Buddhism and Hinduism differ on the concept of self?
response:
Buddhism and Hinduism offer distinct perspectives on the concept of self. In Buddhism, the idea of "not-self" or [anattā](https://siftersearch.com/document/17268) is central, as seen in the teaching that "form is not self" and thus "lends itself to dis-ease," emphasizing the impermanent and non-essential nature of the self, *Samyutta Nikaya*. This view leads to the understanding that attachment to the five aggregates — form, feeling, perception, fabrications, and consciousness — results in ["long-term loss & suffering"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19403), *Samyutta Nikaya*.

In contrast, Hinduism often embraces the concept of a true self or Ātman, which is seen as identical with Brahman, the ultimate reality. The phrase ["Thou art That"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19898), *The Tattva-Muktavali*, reflects this unity, suggesting that the individual soul is fundamentally the same as the universal spirit. This highlights a key difference: while Buddhism denies a permanent self, Hinduism affirms a deeper, unchanging essence.

In Islam, fasting during the month of Ramadan is a time of spiritual reflection and purification, as Rumi poetically describes, ["burbest dehan va dida bogshad"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17096) — closing the mouth and opening the eyes to inner realities. Judaism also regards fasting as a means of repentance and spiritual reflection, as seen in the observance of Yom Kippur, where fasting is a way to ["afflict your souls"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17800) and seek atonement. Hindu texts, like the Mahabharata, suggest that fasting is not merely about abstaining from food but is also about living a life of truth and wisdom, as Bhishma explains, ["he will be regarded as one that is always fasting if he eats once during the day and once during the night"](https://oceanlibrary.com/mahabharata-12_vyasa?paraId=para_1194). In Buddhism, the practice of fasting is linked to the Uposatha, where monks abstain from food at certain times to emulate the arahants and cultivate discipline, as noted in the [Anguttara Nikaya](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutra-collection-tu?paraId=para_1523). Each tradition views fasting as a means to deepen one's spiritual connection and self-discipline.

query: How is prayer described in different traditions?
response:
Prayer is a profound and multifaceted practice across various religious traditions. In Islam, it is said that ["in the remembrance of Alláh do hearts find rest"](https://oceanlibrary.com/meaning-of-the-glorious-quran_muhammad?paraId=para_1774), highlighting the tranquility that comes from prayer. In Judaism, prayer is likened to incense, as expressed in the Psalms: ["Let my prayer be set forth before thee as incense"](https://oceanlibrary.com/book-of-psalms-tehilim_david?paraId=para_2627), symbolizing a fragrant offering to God. Sikhism emphasizes the meditative aspect, urging followers to ["meditate on the Name of the Lord"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20184), which brings honor in the divine court.

query: How do religions explain evil?
response:
Religions offer diverse explanations for the presence of evil, often framing it as a necessary part of the human experience. In Sikhism, the concept of Maya is central, where ["emotional attachment to Maya"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20190) leads to illusion and doubt, causing one to forget the divine, *Guru Granth Sahib - Sri Raag*. The Bahá'í Faith views evil as ["the absence of good"](https://siftersearch.com/document/15205), emphasizing that all creation is inherently good, *Star of the West*. Hinduism addresses evil through the doctrine of Karma, suggesting that actions in past lives influence present circumstances, as seen in the Mahabharata's exploration of ["the karmic evil arising out of sin"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17366), *The Mahabharata - Vana Parva Part 2*.

In Judaism, the concept of the Yetzer Hara, or evil inclination, is seen as a challenge to overcome, with the understanding that it is created by God to bring merit to the righteous, as described in the [*Shemot Rabbah*](https://siftersearch.com/document/18201). Islam recounts the story of Iblis, who refused to prostrate to Adam, illustrating the theme of pride and disobedience as sources of evil, as detailed in the *Stories of the Prophets*. Christianity often interprets evil through the narrative of fallen angels, as St. Augustine describes the division between angels of light and darkness in *City of God and Christian Doctrine*.

The Taoist perspective, as expressed by Zhuangzi, suggests that desires and emotions like joy and anger lead one astray from the Tao, causing a loss of virtue, as noted in [*Chuang Tzu - Mystic, Moralist, and Social Reformer*](https://siftersearch.com/document/18768). Buddhism identifies ignorance as the root of unskillful qualities, with wrong views leading to suffering, as highlighted in the *Sutra Collection (A, Part-2)*. Each tradition offers a unique lens through which to understand and address the concept of evil, emphasizing personal responsibility, spiritual growth, and the pursuit of good.

For further reading, you can explore the *Kitáb-i-Aqdas*, particularly paragraphs 134-137, where Bahá'u'lláh elaborates on the role of justice in society.

query: What Buddhist texts discuss mindfulness, and can you show me a key passage?
response: The *Majjhima Nikaya* offers profound insights into mindfulness, particularly through the *Anapanasati Sutta* which focuses on mindfulness of breathing. It describes how a monk, by training himself to ["breathe in sensitive to the entire body"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17257), remains focused on the body, ardent, alert, and mindful, putting aside greed and distress. This practice is said to lead to the culmination of the four frames of reference, which in turn bring about the seven factors for awakening, ultimately leading to ["clear knowing and release"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17257). This structured approach to mindfulness emphasizes the transformative power of sustained and attentive breathing.

query: Search for passages about the covenant in both the Bahá'í Faith and Judaism
response:
In the Bahá'í Faith, the covenant is central to maintaining unity and guidance. Bahá'u'lláh established a clear covenant by appointing ['Abdu’l-Bahá as the Centre of the Covenant](https://siftersearch.com/document/15200), ensuring that his teachings would be correctly interpreted and followed, as detailed in the *Star of the West*. This covenant is seen as a safeguard against division, with Bahá'u'lláh emphasizing that ["firmness in the Covenant and the Testament"](https://siftersearch.com/document/15207) is crucial for preserving unity, *Star of the West*.

In Judaism, the concept of covenant is foundational, as seen in the Torah where God establishes a covenant with the Israelites. The Book of Hebrews reflects on this, noting that God promised to ["make a new covenant with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-epistle-to-hebrews?paraId=para_109), *Hebrews*. This new covenant is distinct from the one made during the Exodus, highlighting a renewal and deeper relationship with God.

query: What is progressive revelation, and which texts in the library discuss it?
response: Progressive revelation is the concept that divine guidance is revealed progressively over time through different messengers. In the Bahá'í Faith, it is described as a process where ["the religion of God is the One Religion, and all the Prophets have taught it"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bahaullah-and-the-new-era_john-esslemont?paraId=para_719), *Bahá’u’lláh and the New Era*. This idea is echoed in Christianity, where the Bible speaks of a ["new covenant"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-epistle-to-hebrews?paraId=para_109) that builds upon the old, *Hebrews*. In Islam, the Qur'án acknowledges that ["We sent messengers before thee"](https://oceanlibrary.com/meaning-of-the-glorious-quran_muhammad?paraId=para_4331), each bringing guidance appropriate to their time, *The Meaning of the Glorious Qur’án*. Hinduism presents a similar idea through the concept of ["avatárs"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17369), where Vishṇu descends in various forms to restore balance, *The Ramayana of Valmiki*. Sikhism also reflects this through the continuity of the Gurus, as seen in the poetic expression that ["the world met him in Krishna, Buddha; in Christ, in Mohamed"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20196), *The Sisters of the Spinning Wheel - Sikh Poems*. In Judaism, the notion of a new covenant is also present, as God promises to ["put my law in their inward parts"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-book-of-jeremiah?paraId=para_670), *Jeremiah*. Lastly, the Taoist texts emphasize understanding the Great Tâo and its characteristics, which aligns with the idea of evolving understanding, as seen in the progression from ["Benevolence and Righteousness"](https://oceanlibrary.com/texts-of-taoism_chuang-tzu?paraId=para_1560), *The Texts of Taoism*. Each tradition reflects the idea that divine truth unfolds progressively, adapting to humanity's evolving capacity for understanding.

query: True spirituality is really just about being a good person, isn't it?
response:
True spirituality encompasses more than just being a good person; it involves a deeper connection with the divine and the cultivation of inner virtues. In Christianity, Jesus teaches that true righteousness goes beyond outward actions, urging believers to address the ["inward exercise of the mind"](https://oceanlibrary.com/religious-affections_jonathan-edwards?paraId=para_601), *Religious Affections*. Similarly, the Bahá'í writings emphasize that ["deeds, not words, be your adorning"](https://oceanlibrary.com/hidden-words-of-bahaullah?paraId=para_239), *The Hidden Words of Bahá’u’lláh*. In Judaism, Micah distills the essence of the commandments to ["do justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with your God"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18367), *Makkot*. The Tao Te Ching highlights virtues like ["compassion, patience, and humility"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18776), *Tao Te Ching (tr. David Bullen)*, as central to living in harmony with the Tao.

Islamic teachings also stress the importance of sincerity and devotion, where true worship involves ["belief in Divine Unity"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16933), *Sermon of Recognition with Luminousness*. Buddhism teaches that to purify the mind and see the truth, one must ["walk this Way"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17277), *The Buddha's Path of Virtue (Dhammapada tr. Wagiswara and Saunders)*. Sikhism encourages seeing the ["brotherhood of all mankind"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20185), *Guru Granth Sahib - Japji Sahib*, as a path to spiritual victory. In Hinduism, the Upanishads suggest that true understanding comes from a combination of ["knowledge, faith, and the Upanishad"](https://oceanlibrary.com/chandogya-upanishad?paraId=para_14), *Chandogya Upanishad*. Each tradition underscores that while goodness is vital, true spirituality involves a profound inner transformation and connection with the divine.

Furthermore, the Bahá'í teachings recognize that differences in function do not negate equality, as ["equality of men and women is clearly stated"](https://oceanlibrary.com/women_bahaullah?paraId=para_146) in the teachings, *Women*.

query: Progressive revelation is just Bahá'u'lláh saying all religions are basically the same?
response: The idea that progressive revelation means all religions are the same misses a key nuance. Bahá'u'lláh explains that while ["all the Prophets have taught"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bahaullah-and-the-new-era_john-esslemont?paraId=para_719) the same fundamental truth, each revelation is a different stage in the life of the One Religion, akin to a bud, flower, and fruit, *Bahá’u’lláh and the New Era*. This does not mean they are identical; rather, they are stages in a continuous process. The Qur'án, for example, confirms previous scriptures, stating that it was revealed ["with truth, confirming that which was (revealed) before it"](https://oceanlibrary.com/meaning-of-the-glorious-quran_muhammad?paraId=para_306), *The Meaning of the Glorious Qur’án (Pickthall)*. Hindu texts also speak of divine incarnations appearing for the ["well-being of mankind"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19875), *A Prose English Translation of Harivamsha*. These perspectives highlight that while there is a shared divine origin, each religion brings a unique message suited to its time and context.

query: The Bahá'í Faith is basically a liberal religion that supports modern progressive values?
response: Not quite. While the Bahá'í Faith shares some values with modern progressive ideals, such as the ["equality of men and women"](https://siftersearch.com/document/11434) and the ["unity of science and religion"](https://siftersearch.com/document/15206), it also emphasizes principles that go beyond typical liberal frameworks. For instance, Bahá'u'lláh's teachings stress the ["independent investigation of truth"](https://siftersearch.com/document/8680), urging individuals to seek truth beyond inherited traditions. Moreover, the Faith's administrative order is not purely democratic; it is guided by spiritual principles and divine inspiration, as Shoghi Effendi notes that the Universal House of Justice is ["not responsible to those whom they represent"](https://oceanlibrary.com/world-order-of-bahaullah_shoghi-effendi?paraId=para_376). This blend of progressive and unique spiritual principles sets the Bahá'í Faith apart from conventional political categorizations.

query: Isn't the Bahá'í teaching on the soul basically the same as what most people mean by spirituality?
response: Not quite. While the Bahá'í teachings on the soul do share some elements with general spirituality, they offer a distinct perspective. 'Abdu'l-Bahá explains that the human spirit, or rational soul, is unique in its ability to ["discover the realities and become aware of the properties and effects"](https://oceanlibrary.com/some-answered-questions_abdul-baha), *Some Answered Questions*. This rational soul requires the assistance of the spirit of faith to truly comprehend ["divine mysteries and heavenly realities"](https://oceanlibrary.com/some-answered-questions_abdul-baha?paraId=para_728), suggesting a deeper, faith-driven spiritual journey. Moreover, the Bahá'í view emphasizes the soul's eternal progression towards God, a journey that continues beyond physical life, which is not always a focus in broader spiritual discussions.
